title: Conjugate Priors
text: parameter estimation method primary variable interest two-level categorical variable. Supports single variable two-variable (categorical/categorical) cases. application use ‘inz_lbinom’ ‘inz_dbeta’ functions construct prior likelihood object.

title: Conjugate Priors
text: First, construct likelihood object data. likelihood component Bayes’ Theorem, p(x|θ)p(x | \theta). variable, like estimate proportion females (‘successes’) population, θ\theta. Hence, also inherently estimating proportion males (‘failures’), 1-θ\theta, well. Next, set prior, passing likelihood object prior function. prior component Bayes’ Theorem p(θ)p(\theta). start , use uninformative prior, Beta(1,1). default prior set ‘inz_dbeta’ function.  Beta(1,1) = Uniform(0,1). prior, essentially implying don’t prior knowledge θ\theta (proportion females) population. Hence, possible values θ\theta assigned equal probability. Finally, pass prior object contains likelihood prior information calculate_posterior function. function calculates posterior parameters. posterior component Bayes’ Theorem, p(θ|x)p(\theta | x). posterior object used obtain posterior estimates credible interval values summary function. used uninformative prior, estimate θ\theta (1-θ\theta) similar proportions seen data , 0.5350 (107/200) females 0.4650 (93/200) males.
code: head(surf_data$Gender, n=10) ##  [1] "female" "female" "male"   "female" "female" "male"   "female" "male"   ##  [9] "female" "male" likelihood <- inz_lbinom(surf_data, Gender) prior <- inz_dbeta(likelihood) posterior <- calculate_posterior(prior) summary(posterior) ## Estimated Proportions with 95% Credible Interval using a Beta(1,1) prior ##  ##        Estimate  Lower  Upper ## female   0.5347 0.4658 0.6029 ## male     0.4653 0.3971 0.5342

title: Conjugate Priors
text: prior knowledge/belief θ\theta, proportion females population around 0.5 (50%). Let’s change prior informative prior Beta(50,50):  Compared uninformative prior used , prior assigns highest probability θ\theta = 0.5. probability gradually decreases higher lower values θ\theta (0.5). can observe estimate proportion females population, θ\theta, decreased 0.5233 (52.33%) 0.5347 (53.47%) using informative prior.
code: prior <- inz_dbeta(likelihood, alpha = 50, beta = 50) posterior <- calculate_posterior(prior) summary(posterior) ## Estimated Proportions with 95% Credible Interval using a Beta(50,50) prior ##  ##        Estimate  Lower  Upper ## female   0.5233 0.4668 0.5796 ## male     0.4767 0.4204 0.5332

title: Conjugate Priors
text: can set credible level credible interval outputs. done using cred_level argument. default credible level 95%. want 90% credible interval estimates, cred_level=90:  can also round output values (significant figures). done using signif_value argument. output rounded 2 significant figures:
code: posterior <- calculate_posterior(prior, cred_level=90) summary(posterior) ## Estimated Proportions with 90% Credible Interval using a Beta(50,50) prior ##  ##        Estimate  Lower  Upper ## female   0.5233 0.4759 0.5706 ## male     0.4767 0.4294 0.5241 posterior <- calculate_posterior(prior, signif_value=2) summary(posterior) ## Estimated Proportions with 95% Credible Interval using a Beta(50,50) prior ##  ##        Estimate Lower Upper ## female     0.52  0.47  0.58 ## male       0.48  0.42  0.53

title: Conjugate Priors
text: data: two variables, like estimate proportion females (‘successes’) (hence, proportion males (‘failures’)) conditional groups secondary variable (Qualification). words, want estimate proportion Gender group/level Qualification. prior: use Beta(1,1) prior (Uniform prior) groups:  posterior:
code: likelihood <- inz_lbinom(surf_data, Gender, Qualification) prior <- inz_dbeta(likelihood) posterior <- calculate_posterior(prior) summary(posterior) ## Prior ##                      ## degree     Beta(1,1) ## none       Beta(1,1) ## school     Beta(1,1) ## vocational Beta(1,1) ##  ##  ## Estimated Proportions ##  ##            female   male ## degree     0.4000 0.6000 ## none       0.5610 0.4390 ## school     0.5882 0.4118 ## vocational 0.5217 0.4783 ##  ##  ## 95% Credible Intervals ##  ##            female   male ## degree     0.2352 0.4226 ##            0.5774 0.7648 ## none       0.4089 0.2926 ##            0.7074 0.5911 ## school     0.4700 0.2985 ##            0.7015 0.5300 ## vocational 0.4045 0.3622 ##            0.6378 0.5955

title: Conjugate Priors
text: Let’s change prior degree group. prior belief proportion females whose highest qualification degree lower males somewhere around 0.35.
code: prior <- inz_dbeta(likelihood, alpha = c(15,1,1,1), beta = c(27,1,1,1)) posterior <- calculate_posterior(prior) summary(posterior) ## Prior ##                        ## degree     Beta(15,27) ## none       Beta(1,1)   ## school     Beta(1,1)   ## vocational Beta(1,1)   ##  ##  ## Estimated Proportions ##  ##            female   male ## degree     0.3714 0.6286 ## none       0.5610 0.4390 ## school     0.5882 0.4118 ## vocational 0.5217 0.4783 ##  ##  ## 95% Credible Intervals ##  ##            female   male ## degree     0.2629 0.5131 ##            0.4869 0.7371 ## none       0.4089 0.2926 ##            0.7074 0.5911 ## school     0.4700 0.2985 ##            0.7015 0.5300 ## vocational 0.4045 0.3622 ##            0.6378 0.5955

title: Conjugate Priors
text: parameter estimation method primary variable interest multi-level categorical variable. Supports single variable two-variable (categorical/categorical) cases. application use ‘inz_lmulti’ ‘inz_ddir’ functions construct prior likelihood object.

title: Conjugate Priors
text: Using Dirichlet(1,1,1,1) prior (Uniform) posterior:
code: head(surf_data$Qualification, n=10) ##  [1] "school"     "vocational" "none"       "vocational" "school"     ##  [6] "degree"     "none"       "degree"     "vocational" "school" likelihood <- inz_lmulti(surf_data, Qualification) prior <- inz_ddir(likelihood) posterior <- calculate_posterior(prior) summary(posterior) ## Estimated Proportions with 95% Credible Interval using a Dirichlet(1,1,1,1) prior ##  ##            Estimate   Lower  Upper ## degree       0.1422 0.09781 0.1931 ## none         0.1961 0.14470 0.2531 ## school       0.3284 0.26580 0.3942 ## vocational   0.3333 0.27040 0.3993

title: Conjugate Priors
text: likelihood (data) remains Let’s use Jeffreys prior - Dirichlet(0.5,0.5,0.5,0.5):
code: prior <- inz_ddir(likelihood, alpha = rep(0.5, 4)) posterior <- calculate_posterior(prior) summary(posterior) ## Estimated Proportions with 95% Credible Interval using a Dirichlet(0.5,0.5,0.5,0.5) prior ##  ##            Estimate  Lower  Upper ## degree       0.1411 0.0967 0.1922 ## none         0.1955 0.1440 0.2528 ## school       0.3292 0.2662 0.3954 ## vocational   0.3342 0.2709 0.4005

title: Conjugate Priors
code: table(surf_data$Qualification, surf_data$Gender) ##              ##              female male ##   degree         11   17 ##   none           22   17 ##   school         39   27 ##   vocational     35   32 likelihood <- inz_lmulti(surf_data, Qualification, Gender) prior <- inz_ddir(likelihood) posterior <- calculate_posterior(prior) summary(posterior) ## Prior ##                            ## female Dirichlet (1,1,1,1) ## male   Dirichlet (1,1,1,1) ##  ##  ## Estimated Proportions ##  ##        degree   none school vocational ## female 0.1081 0.2072 0.3604     0.3243 ## male   0.1856 0.1856 0.2887     0.3402 ##  ##  ## 95% Credible Intervals ##  ##         degree   none school vocational ## female 0.05765 0.1374 0.2740     0.2408 ##        0.17190 0.2870 0.4515     0.4138 ## male   0.11510 0.1151 0.2033     0.2498 ##        0.26830 0.2683 0.3822     0.4369

title: Conjugate Priors
text: Changing prior
code: likelihood <- inz_lmulti(surf_data, Qualification, Ethnicity) prior <- inz_ddir(likelihood) posterior <- calculate_posterior(prior) summary(posterior) ## Prior ##                              ## European Dirichlet (1,1,1,1) ## Maori    Dirichlet (1,1,1,1) ## other    Dirichlet (1,1,1,1) ## Pacific  Dirichlet (1,1,1,1) ##  ##  ## Estimated Proportions ##  ##           degree    none  school vocational ## European 0.15620 0.19380 0.31880    0.33120 ## Maori    0.10710 0.25000 0.39290    0.25000 ## other    0.17650 0.11760 0.17650    0.52940 ## Pacific  0.09091 0.27270 0.45450    0.18180 ##  ##  ## 95% Credible Intervals ##  ##            degree    none  school vocational ## European 0.104400 0.13650 0.24900    0.26070 ##          0.216200 0.25830 0.39280    0.40580 ## Maori    0.023530 0.11110 0.22390    0.11110 ##          0.242900 0.42260 0.57630    0.42260 ## other    0.040470 0.01551 0.04047    0.29880 ##          0.383500 0.30230 0.38350    0.75350 ## Pacific  0.002529 0.06674 0.18710    0.02521 ##          0.308500 0.55610 0.73760    0.44500 prior <- inz_ddir(likelihood,   alpha = matrix(rep(0.5,16),   ncol=length(likelihood$levels)) ) posterior <- calculate_posterior(prior) summary(posterior) ## Prior ##                                      ## European Dirichlet (0.5,0.5,0.5,0.5) ## Maori    Dirichlet (0.5,0.5,0.5,0.5) ## other    Dirichlet (0.5,0.5,0.5,0.5) ## Pacific  Dirichlet (0.5,0.5,0.5,0.5) ##  ##  ## Estimated Proportions ##  ##           degree    none  school vocational ## European 0.15510 0.19300 0.31960    0.33230 ## Maori    0.09615 0.25000 0.40380    0.25000 ## other    0.16670 0.10000 0.16670    0.56670 ## Pacific  0.05556 0.27780 0.50000    0.16670 ##  ##  ## 95% Credible Intervals ##  ##             degree     none  school vocational ## European 1.031e-01 0.135500 0.24940    0.26120 ##          2.153e-01 0.257900 0.39410    0.40740 ## Maori    1.700e-02 0.106900 0.22750    0.10690 ##          2.327e-01 0.429400 0.59420    0.42940 ## other    3.092e-02 0.007819 0.03092    0.31940 ##          3.849e-01 0.288400 0.38490    0.79710 ## Pacific  5.949e-05 0.055970 0.19900    0.01384 ##          2.622e-01 0.591600 0.80100    0.45370

title: Estimation Methods
text: calculating mean μ\mu variance σ2\sigma^2 single numeric variable yy length nn, using Gibbs sampler. model p(μ,σ2|y)∝p(y|μ,σ2)p(μ)p(σ2)p(\mu,\sigma^2|y) \propto p(y|\mu,\sigma^2) p(\mu) p(\sigma^2) prior μ\mu Normal mean μ0\mu_0 variance σ02\sigma_0^2, prior σ2\sigma^2 Inverse-Gamma parameters α\alpha β\beta. likelihood yy Normal mean μ\mu variance σ2\sigma^2. Since conjugate, full conditionals needed Gibbs sampling p(μ(+1)|σ2,…)=…p(\mu^{(+1)}|\sigma^2, \ldots) = \ldots

title: Bayesian Inference with iNZight
text: goal iNZightBayes lower barrier entry Bayesian analysis providing simple interface Bayesian estimation inference. combined iNZight (GUI) students anyone else just getting started Bayesian analysis can quickly start exploring priors posteriors develop understanding, learning technical coding details.

text: can install released version iNZightBayes CRAN : development version GitHub :

title: Bayesian Inference with iNZight
text: cases, posterior can calculated exactly.  ’s also linear regression:
code: library(iNZightBayes)  post <- estimate_mean(~Sepal.Width, data = iris) summary(post) #>  #> Iterations = 1001:2000 #> Thinning interval = 1  #> Number of chains = 1  #> Sample size per chain = 1000  #>  #> 1. Empirical mean and standard deviation for each variable, #>    plus standard error of the mean: #>  #>          Mean      SD Naive SE Time-series SE #> mu     3.0585 0.03608 0.001141       0.001141 #> sigma2 0.1929 0.02220 0.000702       0.000702 #>  #> 2. Quantiles for each variable: #>  #>          2.5%    25%    50%    75%  97.5% #> mu     2.9854 3.0358 3.0583 3.0819 3.1270 #> sigma2 0.1538 0.1774 0.1912 0.2065 0.2448 plot(post) #> Warning: `aes_string()` was deprecated in ggplot2 3.0.0. #> ℹ Please use tidy evaluation idioms with `aes()`. #> ℹ See also `vignette("ggplot2-in-packages")` for more information. #> ℹ The deprecated feature was likely used in the iNZightBayes package. #>   Please report the issue at <https://github.com/iNZightVIT/iNZightBayes/issues>. #> This warning is displayed once per session. #> Call `lifecycle::last_lifecycle_warnings()` to see where this warning was generated. post <- estimate_proportions(c(20, 50, 30), alpha = c(10, 10, 10)) summary(post) #>          mean       var  2.5% 97.5% #> theta_1 0.231 -4.52e-05 0.164 0.306 #> theta_2 0.462 -3.16e-05 0.376 0.550 #> theta_3 0.308 -4.07e-05 0.230 0.391 plot(post) #> Warning: Using `size` aesthetic for lines was deprecated in ggplot2 3.4.0. #> ℹ Please use `linewidth` instead. #> ℹ The deprecated feature was likely used in the iNZightBayes package. #>   Please report the issue at <https://github.com/iNZightVIT/iNZightBayes/issues>. #> This warning is displayed once per session. #> Call `lifecycle::last_lifecycle_warnings()` to see where this warning was generated. # temporary syntax: y <- iris$Sepal.Length x <- cbind(iris$Sepal.Width) post <- gibbs_lm(y, x, 100)  par(mfrow = c(1, 2)) plot(post$posterior[,1:2]) plot(x[,1], y) apply(post$posterior[,1:2], 1,   function(b)     lines(x[,1], cbind(1, x) %*% b, col = "#00990030")) #> NULL

title: Calculate the Posterior Distribution — calculate_posterior
text: calculate_posterior generic function used calculate posterior distribution given prior associated likelihood.

title: Calculate the Posterior Distribution — calculate_posterior
code: calculate_posterior(prior, ...)  # S3 method for class 'inz_dbeta' calculate_posterior(prior, cred_level = 95, signif_value = 4, ...)  # S3 method for class 'inz_ddir' calculate_posterior(prior, cred_level = 95, signif_value = 4, ...)  # S3 method for class 'inz_dNIG' calculate_posterior(prior, cred_level = 95, signif_value = 4, ...)  # S3 method for class 'inz_dNIG_reg' calculate_posterior(prior, cred_level = 95, signif_value = 4, ...)

title: Calculate the Posterior Distribution — calculate_posterior
text: prior prior object. ... Currently additional arguments. cred_level credible level (%) credible interval (default 95). signif_value number significant figures output (default 4).

title: Calculate the Posterior Distribution — calculate_posterior
text: Returns object class prior. list contains likelihood, prior, posterior parameter values. cred_level signif_value stored attribute.

title: Calculate the Posterior Distribution — calculate_posterior
text: Beta-Binomial conjugacy: posterior parameters inz_dbeta calculated updated using: $$ \begin{aligned} \alpha_{post} &= \alpha_{prior} + x \\ \beta_{post} &= \beta_{prior} + N - x \end{aligned} $$ Dirichlet-Multinomial conjugacy: posterior parameters inz_ddir calculated updated using: $$\boldsymbol{\alpha}_{post} = \boldsymbol{\alpha}_{prior} + \mathbf{x}$$ Normal-Inverse-Gamma Prior, Normal Likelihood conjugacy: posterior parameters inz_dNIG calculated updated using: $$ \begin{aligned} V_n &= \left( \frac{1}{V_0} + n \right)^{-1} \\ m_n &= V_n \left( \frac{m_0}{V_0} + n \bar{x} \right) \\ a_n &= a_0 + \frac{n}{2} \\ b_n &= b_0 + \frac{1}{2} \left(\frac{m_0^2}{V_0} + \sum_{} x_i^2 - \frac{m_n^2}{V_n} \right) \end{aligned} $$ (Murphy, 2007, Section 6.3). Normal-Inverse-Gamma Prior, Normal Likelihood conjugacy regression: posterior parameters inz_dNIG_reg calculated updated using: $$ \begin{aligned} \boldsymbol{\Lambda}_n &= \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} + \boldsymbol{\Lambda}_0 \\ \boldsymbol{\mu}_n &= \boldsymbol{\Lambda}_n^{-1} (\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} \hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} + \boldsymbol{\Lambda}_0 \boldsymbol{\mu}_0) \\ a_n &= a_0 + \frac{n}{2} \\ b_n &= b_0 + \frac{1}{2} (\mathbf{y}^T \mathbf{y} + \boldsymbol{\mu}_0^T \boldsymbol{\Lambda}_0 \boldsymbol{\mu}_0 - \boldsymbol{\mu}_n^T \boldsymbol{\Lambda}_n \boldsymbol{\mu}_n) \end{aligned} $$

title: Calculate the Posterior Distribution — calculate_posterior
text: Murphy, K. P. (2007). Conjugate Bayesian analysis Gaussian distribution (Technical Report). University British Columbia. https://www.cs.ubc.ca/~murphyk/Papers/bayesGauss.pdf

title: Calculate the Posterior Distribution — calculate_posterior
code: # Beta-Binomial example if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ lik <- inz_lbinom(surf_data, Gender) prior <- inz_dbeta(likelihood = lik) calculate_posterior(prior = prior) } # }  # Regression example (Normal-Inverse-Gamma) if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ lik <- inz_lnorm(surf_data, Income, Hours) prior <- inz_dNIG(likelihood = lik) calculate_posterior(prior = prior) } # }

title: Normal-Inverse-Gamma Distribution for Prior and Posterior — inz_dNIG
code: inz_dNIG(   likelihood = NULL,   mu = NULL,   lambda = NULL,   alpha = NULL,   beta = NULL )

title: Normal-Inverse-Gamma Distribution for Prior and Posterior — inz_dNIG
text: likelihood likelihood object class "inz_lnorm". mu normal mean parameter \(\mu\). regression, vector coefficient values. lambda normal precision scaling parameter \(\lambda\). regression, precision matrix \(\Lambda\). alpha inverse-gamma shape parameter \(\alpha\). beta inverse-gamma scale parameter \(\beta\).

title: Normal-Inverse-Gamma Distribution for Prior and Posterior — inz_dNIG
text: Returns object class "inz_dNIG" (single variable/grouped) "inz_dNIG_reg" (regression). object class "inz_dNIG" "inz_dNIG_reg" list contains following: mu mean parameter \(\mu\). lambda precision scale parameter \(\lambda\) precision matrix \(\Lambda\). V variance scale factor covariance matrix. alpha shape parameter \(\alpha\). beta scale parameter \(\beta\). function used construct prior, likelihood stored attribute.

title: Normal-Inverse-Gamma Distribution for Prior and Posterior — inz_dNIG
text: Normal-Inverse-Gamma distribution conjugate prior Normal likelihood, mean variance unknown. \(\lambda > 0\) \(\alpha > 0\) \(\beta > 0\) Single Variable Grouped Data: $$\sigma^2 \sim \Gamma^{-1}(\alpha, \beta)$$ $$\theta | \sigma^2 \sim \text{N}(\mu, \sigma^2V)$$ \(V=1/\lambda\), variance scale factor. single variable, \(\mu\) \(\lambda\) scalars. grouped data, \(\mu\) \(\lambda\) vectors value corresponds group. Regression: $$\sigma^2 \sim \Gamma^{-1}(\alpha, \beta)$$ $$\beta | \sigma^2 \sim \text{N}(\mu, \sigma^2 \Lambda^{-1})$$ regression, \(\mu\) vector length 2 \(\Lambda\) 2x2 precision matrix. prior use : prior parameters provided, uninformative default values used prior: mu = 0 (vector zeros) lambda = 1 (vector ones, identity matrix regression) alpha = 0.001 beta = 0.001

title: Normal-Inverse-Gamma Distribution for Prior and Posterior — inz_dNIG
code: # Constructing the prior with the likelihood (default prior is used) if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ lik <- inz_lnorm(surf_data, Income) inz_dNIG(likelihood = lik)  # Using a subjective prior inz_dNIG(likelihood = lik, mu = 500, lambda = 10, alpha = 5, beta = 1) } # }  # Regression example if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ reg_lik <- inz_lnorm(surf_data, Income, Hours) inz_dNIG(likelihood = reg_lik) } # }  # Example of inz_dNIG usage in the calculate_posterior function inz_dNIG(mu = 572.4975, lambda = 201, alpha = 100.001, beta = 12118391) #> $mu #> [1] 572.4975 #>  #> $lambda #> [1] 201 #>  #> $V #> [1] 0.004975124 #>  #> $alpha #> [1] 100.001 #>  #> $beta #> [1] 12118391 #>  #> attr(,"class") #> [1] "inz_dNIG"

title: Beta Distribution for Prior and Posterior — inz_dbeta

title: Beta Distribution for Prior and Posterior — inz_dbeta
text: Parameter 1: shape \(\alpha > 0\) Parameter 2: shape \(\beta > 0\) Beta distribution conjugate prior (limited ) Binomial likelihood. prior use : alpha beta parameter values provided, default value \(\alpha = 1\) \(\beta = 1\) used respectively. Hence, default prior Beta(1,1) used alpha beta NULL, equivalent uninformative, Uniform(0,1) prior.

title: Beta Distribution for Prior and Posterior — inz_dbeta
code: # Constructing the prior with the likelihood (default prior is used) if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ lik <- inz_lbinom(surf_data, Gender) inz_dbeta(likelihood = lik)  # Using a subjective prior  inz_dbeta(likelihood = lik, alpha = 8, beta = 2) } # }  # Grouped data example if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ grouped_lik <- inz_lbinom(surf_data, Gender, Qualification) inz_dbeta(likelihood = grouped_lik) } # }  # Example of inz_dbeta usage in the calculate_posterior function inz_dbeta(alpha = 108, beta = 94) #> $alpha #> [1] 108 #>  #> $beta #> [1] 94 #>  #> attr(,"class") #> [1] "inz_dbeta"

title: Dirichlet Distribution for Prior and Posterior — inz_ddir
text: shape parameter: \(\alpha_i > 0\); corresponding level/group categorical variable \(k ≥ 2\) Dirichlet distribution conjugate prior (limited ) Multinomial likelihood. data grouped, alpha matrix one row per group. Otherwise, alpha vector. prior use : alpha values provided, default value \(\alpha_i = 1\), used prior (e.g. Dirichlet(1,1,1,1) k=4 level categorical variable).

title: Dirichlet Distribution for Prior and Posterior — inz_ddir
code: # Constructing the prior with the likelihood (default prior is used) if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ lik <- inz_lmulti(surf_data, Qualification) inz_ddir(likelihood = lik)  # Using a subjective prior (prior belief that degree and school qualifications are more common) inz_ddir(likelihood = lik, alpha = c(10, 2, 10, 2)) } # }  # Grouped data example if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ grouped_lik <- inz_lmulti(surf_data, Qualification, Ethnicity) inz_ddir(likelihood = grouped_lik) } # }  # Example of inz_ddir usage in the calculate_posterior function posterior <- inz_ddir(alpha = c(29,40,67,68), k = 4)

what: Construct a Likelihood Object for Normal Data — inz_lnorm
title: Construct a Likelihood Object for Normal Data — inz_lnorm
text: Creates likelihood object numeric variable, optionally grouped categorical variable. , optionally use another numeric variable explore relationship.

title: Construct a Likelihood Object for Normal Data — inz_lnorm
code: inz_lnorm(data, primary, secondary = NULL)

title: Construct a Likelihood Object for Normal Data — inz_lnorm
text: data data frame containing variables. primary primary numeric variable interest. secondary optional secondary variable interest (categorical grouping numeric regression).

title: Construct a Likelihood Object for Normal Data — inz_lnorm
text: Returns object class "inz_lnorm". object class "inz_lnorm" list contains following: single numeric variable: x numeric variable. summary_stat summary statistics variable. x_bar sample mean. n sample size. raw_sum_sq raw sum squares. primary_var name numeric variable. numeric categorical: grouped_data numeric variable grouped categorical variable. summary_stat summary statistics group. x_bar vector sample means group. n vector sample sizes group. raw_sum_sq vector raw sum squares group. primary_var name numeric variable. secondary_var name categorical variable. numeric numeric: x explanatory/independent variable. y response/dependent variable. spearman_correlation Spearman's rank correlation coefficient. x_label name explanatory variable. y_label name response variable.

title: Construct a Likelihood Object for Normal Data — inz_lnorm
text: primary secondary arguments can passed string (e.g. "Height") non-string (e.g. Height). function behaves differently depending input. Single variable - numeric: Computes summary statistics numeric variable. Two variables - numeric categorical: Groups numeric variable categorical variable computes summary statistics group. function automatically identifies variable numeric uses primary variable regardless order variables inputted. Two variables - numeric numeric: Computes Spearman's correlation. secondary variable used explanatory variable primary variable used response variable regression.

title: Construct a Likelihood Object for Normal Data — inz_lnorm
code: # Single variable if (FALSE) inz_lnorm(surf_data, Hours) # \dontrun{}  # Numeric and Categorical (grouped data) if (FALSE) inz_lnorm(surf_data, Income, Qualification) # \dontrun{} if (FALSE) inz_lnorm(surf_data, Qualification, Income) # \dontrun{} # gives the same output  # Numeric and Numeric (regression) if (FALSE) inz_lnorm(surf_data, Income, Hours) # \dontrun{}

title: Posterior Object Summary — posterior_summary
text: object class summary.inz_*, used corresponding print method automatically print output.

title: Posterior Object Summary — posterior_summary
text: Beta-Binomial (inz_dbeta): point estimates calculated using expectation formula Beta distribution: $$\frac{\alpha}{\alpha+\beta}$$ credible intervals calculated using quantile function qbeta. Dirichlet-Multinomial (inz_ddir): point estimates calculated using expectation formula Dirichlet distribution: $$\frac{\alpha_i}{\alpha_0}$$ marginal distribution Dirichlet follows Beta distribution. Hence, credible intervals calculated using quantile function qbeta. Normal-Inverse-Gamma Prior, Normal Likelihood (inz_dNIG inz_dNIG_reg): posterior mean computed calculate_posterior (\(m_n\) \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_n\) regression) point estimates. marginal posterior distribution mean follows t-distribution. Hence, credible intervals calculated using quantile function qt.

title: Posterior Object Summary — posterior_summary
code: # Beta-Binomial example if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ lik <- inz_lbinom(surf_data, Gender) prior <- inz_dbeta(likelihood = lik) posterior <- calculate_posterior(prior = prior) summary(posterior) } # }  # Regression example (Normal-Inverse-Gamma) if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ lik <- inz_lnorm(surf_data, Income, Hours) prior <- inz_dNIG(likelihood = lik) posterior <- calculate_posterior(prior = prior) summary(posterior) } # }
